name: Codec.Bytes

unions.name: Codec
unions.comment: A value that knows how to encode and decode a sequence of bytes.

unions.name: CustomCodec
unions.comment: A partially built `Codec` for a custom type.

unions.name: ObjectCodec
unions.comment: A partially built `Codec` for an object.

aliases.name: Bytes
aliases.comment:
A sequence of bytes. Refer to the [elm/bytes docs][bytes] for more information.

[bytes]: https://package.elm-lang.org/packages/elm/bytes/latest/Bytes#Bytes

aliases.name: Decoder
aliases.comment: Describes how to turn a sequence of bytes into a nice Elm value.

aliases.name: Encoder
aliases.comment: Describes how to generate a sequence of bytes.

aliases.name: Endianness
aliases.comment:
The direction bytes are ordered in memory. Refer to the [elm/bytes docs][endianness] for more information.

[endianness]: https://package.elm-lang.org/packages/elm/bytes/latest/Bytes#Endianness

values.name: array
values.comment: `Codec` between a sequence of bytes and an Elm `Array`.

values.name: bool
values.comment: `Codec` between a sequence of bytes and an Elm `Bool`

values.name: buildCustom
values.comment: Build a `Codec` for a fully specified custom type.

values.name: buildObject
values.comment: Create a `Codec` from a fully specified `ObjectCodec`.

values.name: char
values.comment: `Codec` between a sequence of bytes and an Elm `Char`

values.name: constant
values.comment: Create a `Codec` that encodes nothing and always decodes as the same value.

values.name: custom
values.comment:
Starts building a `Codec` for a custom type.
You need to pass a pattern matching function, see the FAQ for details.

    type Semaphore
        = Red Int String Bool
        | Yellow Float
        | Green

    semaphoreCodec : Codec Semaphore
    semaphoreCodec =
        Codec.custom
            (\fred fyellow fgreen value ->
                case value of
                    Red i s b ->
                        fred i s b

                    Yellow f ->
                        fyellow f

                    Green ->
                        fgreen
            )
            |> Codec.variant3 0 Red Codec.signedInt Codec.string Codec.bool
            |> Codec.variant1 1 Yellow Codec.float64
            |> Codec.variant0 2 Green
            |> Codec.buildCustom

values.name: customWithIdCodec
values.comment:
Same as `custom` but here we can choose what codec to use for the integer id we tell apart variants with.
This is useful if, for example, you know you won't have ids outside of the range 0 - 255 and can use unsignedInt8 instead of the default signedInt32 to save some space.
values.type: Codec.Bytes.Codec Basics.Int -> match -> Codec.Bytes.CustomCodec match value

values.name: decodeValue
values.comment: Run a `Codec` to turn a sequence of bytes into an Elm value.

values.name: decoder
values.comment: Extracts the `Decoder` contained inside the `Codec`.

values.name: dict
values.comment: `Codec` between a sequence of bytes and an Elm `Dict`.
values.type: Codec.Bytes.Codec comparable -> Codec.Bytes.Codec a -> Codec.Bytes.Codec (Dict.Dict comparable a)

values.name: encodeToValue
values.comment: Convert an Elm value into a sequence of bytes.

values.name: encoder
values.comment: Extracts the encoding function contained inside the `Codec`.

values.name: field
values.comment: Specify how to get a value from the object we want to encode and then give a `Codec` for that value.
values.type: (a -> f) -> Codec.Bytes.Codec f -> Codec.Bytes.ObjectCodec a (f -> b) -> Codec.Bytes.ObjectCodec a b

values.name: float32
values.comment:
`Codec` between a 32-bit float and an Elm `Float`.
Due to Elm `Float`s being 64-bit, encoding and decoding it as a 32-bit float won't exactly equal the original value.

values.name: float64
values.comment: `Codec` between a 64-bit float and an Elm `Float`

values.name: list
values.comment: `Codec` between a sequence of bytes and an Elm `List`.

values.name: map
values.comment: Transform a `Codec`.
values.type: (a -> b) -> (b -> a) -> Codec.Bytes.Codec a -> Codec.Bytes.Codec b

values.name: maybe
values.comment: Represents an optional value.

values.name: object
values.comment:
Start creating a `Codec` for an object. You should pass the main constructor as argument.
If you don't have one (for example it's a simple type with no name), you should pass a function that given the field values builds an object.

    type alias Point =
        { x : Int
        , y : Int
        }

    pointCodec : Codec Point
    pointCodec =
        Codec.object Point
            |> Codec.field .x Codec.signedInt
            |> Codec.field .y Codec.signedInt
            |> Codec.buildObject

values.name: recursive
values.comment:
Create a `Codec` for a recursive data structure.
The argument to the function you need to pass is the fully formed `Codec`, see the FAQ for details.

    type Peano
        = Peano (Maybe Peano)

    peanoCodec : Codec Peano
    peanoCodec =
        Codec.recursive
            (\finishedCodec ->
                Codec.maybe finishedCodec
                    |> Codec.map Peano (\(Peano p) -> p)
            )

values.name: result
values.comment: `Codec` for `Result` values.
values.type: Codec.Bytes.Codec error -> Codec.Bytes.Codec value -> Codec.Bytes.Codec (Result.Result error value)

values.name: set
values.comment: `Codec` between a sequence of bytes and an Elm `Set`.

values.name: signedInt
values.comment:
`Codec` between a signed 32-bit integer and an Elm `Int`.
Use this if the byte ordering and number of bytes used isn't a concern.

values.name: signedInt16
values.comment: `Codec` between a signed 16-bit integer and an Elm `Int`

values.name: signedInt32
values.comment: `Codec` between a signed 32-bit integer and an Elm `Int`

values.name: signedInt8
values.comment: `Codec` between a signed 8-bit integer and an Elm `Int`

values.name: string
values.comment: `Codec` between a sequence of bytes and an Elm `String`

values.name: triple
values.comment: `Codec` between a sequence of bytes and an Elm triple.
values.type: Codec.Bytes.Codec a -> Codec.Bytes.Codec b -> Codec.Bytes.Codec c -> Codec.Bytes.Codec ( a, b, c )

values.name: tuple
values.comment: `Codec` between a sequence of bytes and an Elm `Tuple`.
values.type: Codec.Bytes.Codec a -> Codec.Bytes.Codec b -> Codec.Bytes.Codec ( a, b )

values.name: unsignedInt
values.comment:
`Codec` between an unsigned 32-bit integer and an Elm `Int`.
Use this if the byte ordering and number of bytes used isn't a concern.

values.name: unsignedInt16
values.comment: `Codec` between an unsigned 16-bit integer and an Elm `Int`

values.name: unsignedInt32
values.comment: `Codec` between an unsigned 32-bit integer and an Elm `Int`

values.name: unsignedInt8
values.comment: `Codec` between an unsigned 8-bit integer and an Elm `Int`

values.name: variant0
values.comment: Define a variant with 0 parameters for a custom type.
values.type: Basics.Int -> v -> Codec.Bytes.CustomCodec (Codec.Bytes.Encoder -> a) v -> Codec.Bytes.CustomCodec a v

values.name: variant1
values.comment: Define a variant with 1 parameters for a custom type.
values.type: Basics.Int -> (a -> v) -> Codec.Bytes.Codec a -> Codec.Bytes.CustomCodec ((a -> Codec.Bytes.Encoder) -> b) v -> Codec.Bytes.CustomCodec b v

values.name: variant2
values.comment: Define a variant with 2 parameters for a custom type.
values.type: Basics.Int -> (a -> b -> v) -> Codec.Bytes.Codec a -> Codec.Bytes.Codec b -> Codec.Bytes.CustomCodec ((a -> b -> Codec.Bytes.Encoder) -> c) v -> Codec.Bytes.CustomCodec c v

values.name: variant3
values.comment: Define a variant with 3 parameters for a custom type.
values.type: Basics.Int -> (a -> b -> c -> v) -> Codec.Bytes.Codec a -> Codec.Bytes.Codec b -> Codec.Bytes.Codec c -> Codec.Bytes.CustomCodec ((a -> b -> c -> Codec.Bytes.Encoder) -> partial) v -> Codec.Bytes.CustomCodec partial v

values.name: variant4
values.comment: Define a variant with 4 parameters for a custom type.
values.type: Basics.Int -> (a -> b -> c -> d -> v) -> Codec.Bytes.Codec a -> Codec.Bytes.Codec b -> Codec.Bytes.Codec c -> Codec.Bytes.Codec d -> Codec.Bytes.CustomCodec ((a -> b -> c -> d -> Codec.Bytes.Encoder) -> partial) v -> Codec.Bytes.CustomCodec partial v

values.name: variant5
values.comment: Define a variant with 5 parameters for a custom type.
values.type: Basics.Int -> (a -> b -> c -> d -> e -> v) -> Codec.Bytes.Codec a -> Codec.Bytes.Codec b -> Codec.Bytes.Codec c -> Codec.Bytes.Codec d -> Codec.Bytes.Codec e -> Codec.Bytes.CustomCodec ((a -> b -> c -> d -> e -> Codec.Bytes.Encoder) -> partial) v -> Codec.Bytes.CustomCodec partial v

values.name: variant6
values.comment: Define a variant with 6 parameters for a custom type.
values.type: Basics.Int -> (a -> b -> c -> d -> e -> f -> v) -> Codec.Bytes.Codec a -> Codec.Bytes.Codec b -> Codec.Bytes.Codec c -> Codec.Bytes.Codec d -> Codec.Bytes.Codec e -> Codec.Bytes.Codec f -> Codec.Bytes.CustomCodec ((a -> b -> c -> d -> e -> f -> Codec.Bytes.Encoder) -> partial) v -> Codec.Bytes.CustomCodec partial v

values.name: variant7
values.comment: Define a variant with 7 parameters for a custom type.
values.type: Basics.Int -> (a -> b -> c -> d -> e -> f -> g -> v) -> Codec.Bytes.Codec a -> Codec.Bytes.Codec b -> Codec.Bytes.Codec c -> Codec.Bytes.Codec d -> Codec.Bytes.Codec e -> Codec.Bytes.Codec f -> Codec.Bytes.Codec g -> Codec.Bytes.CustomCodec ((a -> b -> c -> d -> e -> f -> g -> Codec.Bytes.Encoder) -> partial) v -> Codec.Bytes.CustomCodec partial v

values.name: variant8
values.comment: Define a variant with 8 parameters for a custom type.
values.type: Basics.Int -> (a -> b -> c -> d -> e -> f -> g -> h -> v) -> Codec.Bytes.Codec a -> Codec.Bytes.Codec b -> Codec.Bytes.Codec c -> Codec.Bytes.Codec d -> Codec.Bytes.Codec e -> Codec.Bytes.Codec f -> Codec.Bytes.Codec g -> Codec.Bytes.Codec h -> Codec.Bytes.CustomCodec ((a -> b -> c -> d -> e -> f -> g -> h -> Codec.Bytes.Encoder) -> partial) v -> Codec.Bytes.CustomCodec partial v
